title: Optimal quantum locally differentially private mechanisms in the high-privacy regime
abstract: We optimize the trade-off between privacy and utility in the high-privacy regime. We adopt local differential privacy (LDP) and its quantum extension, quantum local differential privacy (QLDP), for privacy protection, and investigate utility functions including the Holevo information (which reduces to the mutual information in the classical case) and the error exponents in symmetric and asymmetric hypothesis testing. These utility functions have classical and quantum optimal values, which are denoted by $C$ and $Q$, respectively, in this abstract for simplicity. In this paper, we provide optimal LDP and QLDP mechanisms achieving the classical and quantum optimal values in the high-privacy regime, and prove that the asymptotic ratio $Q/C$ in this regime takes the same value regardless of the utility function. Our results reveal quantum advantages (more precisely, $Q/C\ge3/2$) for the above utility functions when the protected private data are $n$-ary with $n\ge3$.
summary: This paper identifies a fundamental quantum advantage in the high-privacy regime of local differential privacy. It proves that for datasets with three or more items, quantum mechanisms provide a strictly higher utility-to-privacy ratio than classical mechanisms, regardless of the specific information-theoretic metric used.
main_problem: Optimizing the trade-off between privacy and utility in the high-privacy regime (epsilon -> 0) for Quantum Local Differential Privacy (QLDP) compared to classical LDP.
main_result: The paper proves that in the high-privacy regime, the ratio of quantum to classical optimal utility (Q/C) is constant regardless of the utility function and demonstrates a quantum advantage of at least 3/2 for data with cardinality n >= 3.
method: The author uses second-order Taylor expansions, Fréchet derivatives, and Petz monotone metrics to analyze utility functions, alongside frame theory (isoclinic mechanisms) to construct optimal quantum mechanisms.
model_or_system: The framework involves a data collector estimating information from private data provided via quantum states (QLDP) or classical probability distributions (LDP), using density matrices on a d-dimensional Hilbert space.
key_observables: Holevo information, mutual information, error exponents in symmetric and asymmetric hypothesis testing, and f-divergences.
important_parameters: Privacy parameter epsilon (specifically the limit epsilon -> 0), data cardinality n, and the dimension d of the Hilbert space.
main_assumptions: The utility functions are assumed to be symmetric, unitary invariant, and possess a positive second derivative (Hessian) at the uniform distribution; the quantum advantage disappears for binary data (n=2).
figures_summary: Figure 1 plots the ratios of symmetric and asymmetric error exponents against epsilon for different n, illustrating the presence of quantum advantage for small epsilon.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the privacy-utility trade-off, establishes the mathematical framework for LDP and QLDP, proves the fundamental asymptotic ratio theorem, constructs optimal isoclinic mechanisms, and analyzes specific hypothesis testing scenarios.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Graphs of S1(⃗σ∗)/S1 C(n, ε) and A1(⃗σ∗)/A1 C(n, ε). The hori- zontal axes represent the values of ε, and the vertical axes represent the values of the two ratios. The black dotted lines, the blue dashed lines, and the red solid lines are the cases n = 3, n = 6, and n = 10, respec- tively.

Figure 2. Graphs of S1(⃗σ)/S1 C(10, ε) and A1(⃗σ)/A1 C(10, ε). The hori- zontal axes represent the values of ε, and the vertical axes represent the values of the two ratios. The red solid lines and the red dotted lines are the cases ⃗σ = ⃗σ∗and ⃗σ = ⃗σ∗∗, respectively.
